  3. Week 1

STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis

Week 1: Module Introduction & Time Series Overview

📝 Annotation space: Use the callout blocks and blank lines throughout this guide to add your own notes after reviewing the material.

1 Key Topics Covered This Week

  • What is a Time Series and why it matters
  • Module overview: learning outcomes, assessments, and schedule
  • Components of a Time Series: Trend, Seasonality, Cyclical, Residual
  • Decomposition models: Additive vs. Multiplicative
  • Moving Average (MA) for trend estimation
  • Centred Moving Average (CMA)
  • Calculating Seasonal Indices (corrected)
  • Introduction to ARIMA / SARIMA models (big picture)
  • Software: R / RStudio and Excel (used in lab this week)
  • Practical lab: Manual decomposition in Excel (Exercises 1–3)

2 Core Explanation

2.1 What is a Time Series?

A time series is a sequence of data points recorded at regular intervals over time — for example, daily attendance figures, quarterly income, or monthly airline passengers. The defining feature is that the order of observations matters: what happened yesterday influences what happens today.

The core goal of time series analysis is to:

  1. Understand the historical behaviour of the data
  2. Model the underlying patterns mathematically
  3. Forecast future values with measurable confidence

📝 Your notes here:

2.2 The Four Components of a Time Series

Every time series can be broken down into four building blocks:

Component Symbol What it represents
Trend T The long-run direction (upward, downward, or flat)
Seasonality S Regular, repeating patterns within a fixed period (e.g., every week, every year)
Cyclical C Longer-term waves not tied to a fixed period (e.g., economic boom/bust cycles)
Residual / Remainder R (or ε) What is left after removing the other components — random noise

Example from the lecture: A drug sales dataset (used in the textbook) shows:

  • An upward trend over 30 years
  • A seasonal pattern: sales rise and fall in the same shape each year
  • Residuals that contain random fluctuation

📝 Your notes here:

2.3 Decomposition Models: Additive vs. Multiplicative

How you combine the components determines your decomposition model.

2.3.1 Additive Model

Use this when the size of the seasonal swings stays roughly constant over time (homogeneous variance).

\[ y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t \]

Therefore, the residual is:

\[ R_t = y_t - T_t - S_t \]

2.3.2 Multiplicative Model

Use this when the seasonal swings grow or shrink proportionally with the trend (heterogeneous variance / heteroscedasticity). A log transformation can convert this to an additive form.

\[ y_t = T_t \times S_t \times R_t \]

Therefore, the residual is:

\[ R_t = \frac{y_t}{T_t \times S_t} \]

How to decide which model? Look at the time series plot. If the peaks and troughs get wider as the trend increases → Multiplicative. If they stay the same width → Additive.

📝 Your notes here:

2.4 Moving Average (MA) — Estimating the Trend

A Moving Average smooths out the short-term noise to reveal the underlying trend. You take a window of consecutive values, average them, and slide the window one step forward.

For a periodicity of \(p\), the moving average at time \(t\) is:

\[ MA_t = \frac{1}{p} \sum_{i=0}^{p-1} y_{t-i} \]

From the Excel lab (Ex1 — Weekly Attendance, p = 5):

The data records daily attendance (Mon–Fri) for 3 weeks. A 5-point moving average is computed:

MA = (Mon + Tue + Wed + Thu + Fri) / 5

This strips out the day-of-week seasonal pattern, leaving a cleaner picture of the trend.

📝 Your notes here:

2.5 Centred Moving Average (CMA)

When the period \(p\) is even (e.g., \(p = 4\) for quarterly data), the simple MA falls between time points. To realign it with actual observations, you compute the Centred Moving Average:

\[ CMA_t = \frac{MA_t + MA_{t+1}}{2} \]

From the Excel lab (Ex2 — Quarterly Income, p = 4):

Because there are 4 quarters, the 4-point MA sits between Q2 and Q3. Averaging two consecutive MAs centres it on Q3 of each year.

📝 Your notes here:

2.6 Seasonal Index — Measuring the Seasonal Effect

The seasonal index quantifies how much each period (e.g., each day of the week, each quarter) systematically differs from the average.

2.6.1 Step-by-step (Additive):

  1. Compute CMA (the trend estimate)
  2. Calculate deviations: \(y_t - CMA_t\)
  3. Average the deviations for each season across all years → raw seasonal index
  4. Correct the indices so they sum to zero:

\[ \text{Corrected Index} = \text{Raw Index} - \bar{\text{Index}} \]

2.6.2 Step-by-step (Multiplicative):

  1. Compute CMA
  2. Calculate ratios: \(y_t / CMA_t\)
  3. Average ratios per season → raw index
  4. Correct so they average to 1 (i.e., sum = number of seasons):

\[ \text{Corrected Index} = \frac{\text{Raw Index}}{\bar{\text{Index}}} \]

📝 Your notes here:

2.7 Big Picture: ARIMA and SARIMA

The lecturer gave a preview of where the course is heading. The classical decomposition methods (above) are intuitive but limited. The more powerful statistical approach is the SARIMA framework:

\[ \text{SARIMA}(p, d, q)(P, D, Q)_s \]

  • AR(p): AutoRegressive — today’s value depends on the past \(p\) values
  • I(d): Integrated — we difference the series \(d\) times to achieve stationarity
  • MA(q): Moving Average — today’s value depends on past \(q\) residual errors
  • The seasonal \((P, D, Q)_s\) terms do the same for the seasonal pattern

Building SARIMA requires first achieving stationarity (constant mean and variance). This will be covered in detail in later weeks.

📝 Your notes here:

3 Key Equations / Formulas

Additive model:         y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t
Multiplicative model:   y_t = T_t × S_t × R_t

Moving Average (odd p): MA_t = (1/p) × Σ y_{t-i}

Centred MA (even p):    CMA_t = (MA_t + MA_{t+1}) / 2

Additive residual:      R_t = y_t - T_t - S_t
Multiplicative residual: R_t = y_t / (T_t × S_t)

Seasonal index correction (additive):  Corrected = Raw - Mean(Raw indices)
Seasonal index correction (multiplicative): Corrected = Raw / Mean(Raw indices)

📝 Your notes here:

4 Practical Examples (Excel Lab)

Three exercises were set up in Lecture_1_CIT_template_SOLUTION_9Feb.xlsx:

4.1 Exercise 1 — Daily Attendance (p = 5, Additive)

  • Data: Mon–Fri attendance over 3 weeks
  • Task: Compute 5-point MA, seasonal index per day, and residuals
  • Key insight: Friday has a consistently negative index (~-8.5), while Monday/Tuesday are positive. This makes intuitive sense — attendance drops at the end of the week.

4.2 Exercise 2 — Quarterly Income (p = 4)

  • Data: Quarterly income figures from 2007–2010 across four years
  • Tabs:
    • Ex2 (A) — Additive decomposition (Y = T + S + R)
    • Ex2 (m) — Multiplicative decomposition (Y = T × S × R)
    • Ex2 (A v M) — Side-by-side comparison of both approaches
  • Key insight: The income series shows heteroscedasticity (variance changes over time), suggesting the multiplicative model is more appropriate. This can also be addressed with a log(y_t) transformation.

4.3 Exercises 3 (exerc1–exerc3) — Practice datasets

Three blank templates for you to practise decomposition independently:

Sheet Variable Period
exerc1 Airline Passengers Quarterly, 2008–2010
exerc2 Fuel Quarterly, 2008–2011
exerc3 PC Sales Quarterly, 2009–2011

📝 Your notes here:

5 Slides to Review

Based on the lecture transcript and materials, here is a checklist of the presentation slides from STAT9005_TS_Module_Presentation.pdf and what was covered:

Slide Title Status
1 Title slide ✅ Covered
2 Module Scope – Learning Outcomes (LO1–LO6) ✅ Covered in depth
3 Module Timetable ✅ Note: classes moved to Monday (update pending)
4 Module Contents (overview) ✅ Covered
5 Module Contents – Time Series (10-week breakdown) ✅ Covered
6 Assessments Breakdown ✅ Covered
7 Software (R + RStudio) ✅ Mentioned — install for Week 3
8 Further Resources — Books ✅ Covered
9 Further Resources — Web Links ✅ Covered

5.0.1 ⚠️ Slides NOT in the uploaded PDF (mentioned in lecture — check Canvas)

The lecturer referenced additional visual slides during the walkthrough of concepts that appear to be from a separate content presentation (not the module intro PDF). Look on Canvas for slides covering:

These are likely in a separate “Lecture 1 content” slide deck — the uploaded PDF was only the module introduction/overview.

6 Summary Points

  • A time series is data collected over regular time intervals; order matters
  • Every series can be decomposed into Trend + Seasonality + Cyclical + Residual
  • Use an Additive model (Y = T + S + R) when variance is stable; use a Multiplicative model (Y = T × S × R) when variance grows with the trend
  • Moving Average estimates the trend by smoothing; use Centred MA for even-period data
  • Seasonal indices measure how each season deviates from average — they must sum to zero (additive) or average to 1 (multiplicative)
  • The residuals (remainder) are used to build confidence intervals for forecasts
  • The ultimate goal this semester: build SARIMA models for forecasting
  • First 2 weeks: Excel-based manual decomposition to build intuition
  • From Week 3 onwards: R / RStudio for all analysis
  • Assessment: 20% MCQ test + 40% R practical + 40% project/presentation (no final exam)

7 Resources

Resource Link
Online Textbook (3rd ed.) https://otexts.com/fpp3/
Online Textbook (2nd ed.) https://otexts.com/fpp2/
TDS Article (full TS walkthrough) https://towardsdatascience.com/the-complete-guide-to-time-series-analysis-and-forecasting-70d476bfe775
R Project https://www.r-project.org/
RStudio IDE https://www.rstudio.com/
Tidyverse https://www.tidyverse.org/
ggplot2 gallery https://www.r-graph-gallery.com/
R Markdown https://rmarkdown.rstudio.com

End of Week 1 Study Guide — STAT9005 Time Series Analysis

Weekly Notes
Week 2
Source Code
---
title: "STAT9005 – Time Series Analysis"
subtitle: "Week 1: Module Introduction & Time Series Overview"
---

> 📝 **Annotation space:** Use the callout blocks and blank lines throughout this guide to add your own notes after reviewing the material.

---

# Key Topics Covered This Week

- What is a **Time Series** and why it matters
- **Module overview**: learning outcomes, assessments, and schedule
- **Components of a Time Series**: Trend, Seasonality, Cyclical, Residual
- **Decomposition models**: Additive vs. Multiplicative
- **Moving Average (MA)** for trend estimation
- **Centred Moving Average (CMA)**
- Calculating **Seasonal Indices** (corrected)
- Introduction to **ARIMA / SARIMA** models (big picture)
- Software: **R / RStudio** and **Excel** (used in lab this week)
- Practical lab: Manual decomposition in Excel (Exercises 1–3)

---

# Core Explanation

## What is a Time Series?

A **time series** is a sequence of data points recorded at regular intervals over time — for example, daily attendance figures, quarterly income, or monthly airline passengers. The defining feature is that the **order of observations matters**: what happened yesterday influences what happens today.

The core goal of time series analysis is to:

1. **Understand** the historical behaviour of the data
2. **Model** the underlying patterns mathematically
3. **Forecast** future values with measurable confidence

> 📝 *Your notes here:*

---

## The Four Components of a Time Series

Every time series can be broken down into four building blocks:

| Component | Symbol | What it represents |
|-----------|--------|--------------------|
| **Trend** | T | The long-run direction (upward, downward, or flat) |
| **Seasonality** | S | Regular, repeating patterns within a fixed period (e.g., every week, every year) |
| **Cyclical** | C | Longer-term waves not tied to a fixed period (e.g., economic boom/bust cycles) |
| **Residual / Remainder** | R (or ε) | What is left after removing the other components — random noise |

**Example from the lecture:** A drug sales dataset (used in the textbook) shows:

- An upward **trend** over 30 years
- A **seasonal** pattern: sales rise and fall in the same shape each year
- **Residuals** that contain random fluctuation

> 📝 *Your notes here:*

---

## Decomposition Models: Additive vs. Multiplicative

How you combine the components determines your **decomposition model**.

### Additive Model

Use this when the **size of the seasonal swings stays roughly constant** over time (homogeneous variance).

$$
y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t
$$

Therefore, the residual is:

$$
R_t = y_t - T_t - S_t
$$

### Multiplicative Model

Use this when the **seasonal swings grow or shrink proportionally** with the trend (heterogeneous variance / heteroscedasticity). A log transformation can convert this to an additive form.

$$
y_t = T_t \times S_t \times R_t
$$

Therefore, the residual is:

$$
R_t = \frac{y_t}{T_t \times S_t}
$$

**How to decide which model?** Look at the time series plot. If the peaks and troughs get wider as the trend increases → **Multiplicative**. If they stay the same width → **Additive**.

> 📝 *Your notes here:*

---

## Moving Average (MA) — Estimating the Trend

A **Moving Average** smooths out the short-term noise to reveal the underlying trend. You take a window of consecutive values, average them, and slide the window one step forward.

For a **periodicity of $p$**, the moving average at time $t$ is:

$$
MA_t = \frac{1}{p} \sum_{i=0}^{p-1} y_{t-i}
$$

**From the Excel lab (Ex1 — Weekly Attendance, p = 5):**

The data records daily attendance (Mon–Fri) for 3 weeks. A 5-point moving average is computed:

```
MA = (Mon + Tue + Wed + Thu + Fri) / 5
```

This strips out the day-of-week seasonal pattern, leaving a cleaner picture of the trend.

> 📝 *Your notes here:*

---

## Centred Moving Average (CMA)

When the **period $p$ is even** (e.g., $p = 4$ for quarterly data), the simple MA falls *between* time points. To realign it with actual observations, you compute the **Centred Moving Average**:

$$
CMA_t = \frac{MA_t + MA_{t+1}}{2}
$$

**From the Excel lab (Ex2 — Quarterly Income, p = 4):**

Because there are 4 quarters, the 4-point MA sits between Q2 and Q3. Averaging two consecutive MAs centres it on Q3 of each year.

> 📝 *Your notes here:*

---

## Seasonal Index — Measuring the Seasonal Effect

The **seasonal index** quantifies how much each period (e.g., each day of the week, each quarter) systematically differs from the average.

### Step-by-step (Additive):

1. Compute CMA (the trend estimate)
2. Calculate deviations: $y_t - CMA_t$
3. Average the deviations for each season across all years → raw seasonal index
4. **Correct** the indices so they sum to zero:

$$
\text{Corrected Index} = \text{Raw Index} - \bar{\text{Index}}
$$

### Step-by-step (Multiplicative):

1. Compute CMA
2. Calculate ratios: $y_t / CMA_t$
3. Average ratios per season → raw index
4. **Correct** so they average to 1 (i.e., sum = number of seasons):

$$
\text{Corrected Index} = \frac{\text{Raw Index}}{\bar{\text{Index}}}
$$

> 📝 *Your notes here:*

---

## Big Picture: ARIMA and SARIMA

The lecturer gave a preview of where the course is heading. The classical decomposition methods (above) are intuitive but limited. The more powerful statistical approach is the **SARIMA** framework:

$$
\text{SARIMA}(p, d, q)(P, D, Q)_s
$$

- **AR(p)**: AutoRegressive — today's value depends on the past $p$ values
- **I(d)**: Integrated — we difference the series $d$ times to achieve stationarity
- **MA(q)**: Moving Average — today's value depends on past $q$ residual errors
- The seasonal $(P, D, Q)_s$ terms do the same for the seasonal pattern

Building SARIMA requires first achieving **stationarity** (constant mean and variance). This will be covered in detail in later weeks.

> 📝 *Your notes here:*

---

# Key Equations / Formulas

```
Additive model:         y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t
Multiplicative model:   y_t = T_t × S_t × R_t

Moving Average (odd p): MA_t = (1/p) × Σ y_{t-i}

Centred MA (even p):    CMA_t = (MA_t + MA_{t+1}) / 2

Additive residual:      R_t = y_t - T_t - S_t
Multiplicative residual: R_t = y_t / (T_t × S_t)

Seasonal index correction (additive):  Corrected = Raw - Mean(Raw indices)
Seasonal index correction (multiplicative): Corrected = Raw / Mean(Raw indices)
```

> 📝 *Your notes here:*

---

# Practical Examples (Excel Lab)

Three exercises were set up in `Lecture_1_CIT_template_SOLUTION_9Feb.xlsx`:

## Exercise 1 — Daily Attendance (p = 5, Additive)

- **Data:** Mon–Fri attendance over 3 weeks
- **Task:** Compute 5-point MA, seasonal index per day, and residuals
- **Key insight:** Friday has a consistently negative index (~-8.5), while Monday/Tuesday are positive. This makes intuitive sense — attendance drops at the end of the week.

## Exercise 2 — Quarterly Income (p = 4)

- **Data:** Quarterly income figures from 2007–2010 across four years
- **Tabs:**
  - `Ex2 (A)` — Additive decomposition (Y = T + S + R)
  - `Ex2 (m)` — Multiplicative decomposition (Y = T × S × R)
  - `Ex2 (A v M)` — Side-by-side comparison of both approaches
- **Key insight:** The income series shows heteroscedasticity (variance changes over time), suggesting the **multiplicative model** is more appropriate. This can also be addressed with a `log(y_t)` transformation.

## Exercises 3 (exerc1–exerc3) — Practice datasets

Three blank templates for you to practise decomposition independently:

| Sheet | Variable | Period |
|-------|----------|--------|
| exerc1 | Airline Passengers | Quarterly, 2008–2010 |
| exerc2 | Fuel | Quarterly, 2008–2011 |
| exerc3 | PC Sales | Quarterly, 2009–2011 |

> 📝 *Your notes here:*

---

# Slides to Review

Based on the lecture transcript and materials, here is a checklist of the **presentation slides** from `STAT9005_TS_Module_Presentation.pdf` and what was covered:

| Slide | Title | Status |
|-------|-------|--------|
| 1 | Title slide | ✅ Covered |

### ⚠️ Slides NOT in the uploaded PDF (mentioned in lecture — check Canvas)

The lecturer referenced additional visual slides during the walkthrough of concepts that appear to be from a **separate content presentation** (not the module intro PDF). Look on Canvas for slides covering:

- [ ] Time series plot of **drug/anti-diabetic sales** (trend + seasonality illustration)
- [ ] **Scatter plot / ACF / PACF** of beer sales (correlation with 4-period lags)
- [ ] **Regression model prediction chart** (blue trend line + confidence interval shading)
- [ ] **Time series decomposition diagram** (T, S, R components visualised separately)
- [ ] **Holt-Winters exponential smoothing** model diagram
- [ ] **Stationarity illustration** (constant mean + constant variance plots)
- [ ] **Differencing transformation** example (non-stationary → stationary)
- [ ] **ARIMA forecast plot** with confidence intervals

> These are likely in a separate "Lecture 1 content" slide deck — the uploaded PDF was only the module introduction/overview.

---

# Summary Points

- A time series is data collected over regular time intervals; **order matters**
- Every series can be decomposed into **Trend + Seasonality + Cyclical + Residual**
- Use an **Additive model** (Y = T + S + R) when variance is stable; use a **Multiplicative model** (Y = T × S × R) when variance grows with the trend
- **Moving Average** estimates the trend by smoothing; use **Centred MA** for even-period data
- **Seasonal indices** measure how each season deviates from average — they must sum to zero (additive) or average to 1 (multiplicative)
- The **residuals** (remainder) are used to build **confidence intervals** for forecasts
- The ultimate goal this semester: build **SARIMA** models for forecasting
- **First 2 weeks:** Excel-based manual decomposition to build intuition
- **From Week 3 onwards:** R / RStudio for all analysis
- **Assessment:** 20% MCQ test + 40% R practical + 40% project/presentation (no final exam)

---
